question: GaryClaw on main branch. You have three pieces of work in flight: Phase 4b (cron scheduling), the Creative Oracle design (memory-backed decisions), and Parallel Daemons (multi-instance support). What should this CEO review focus on? If you pick a single design, I'll deep-dive it. If you pick 'all three,' I'll review them as a unified roadmap.

options.label: All three as roadmap (Recommended)
options.description: CEO review of the full next-phase strategy: 4b + Creative Oracle + Parallel Daemons as a unified vision. Best for prioritization and sequencing decisions.

options.label: Creative Oracle only
options.description: Deep-dive the memory-backed decision engine design (Phase 5). Already has a detailed draft.

options.label: Parallel Daemons only
options.description: Deep-dive multi-instance daemon support. Already has a detailed draft.

options.label: Phase 4b only
options.description: Focus on cron triggers and config hot-reload — the stated 'next step' in CLAUDE.md.

rationale: CEO review is a prioritization and sequencing exercise — reviewing all three together lets you make sequencing decisions, spot dependencies, and cut scope in one pass rather than three separate reviews.

question: Three ways to sequence this work. Think of it like building a race car: Approach A builds the engine first (scheduling + parallelism = infrastructure), Approach B builds the brain first (Oracle memory = intelligence), Approach C builds everything together as one integrated system. Which approach fits your priorities?

options.label: B) Brain-First (Recommended)
options.description: Creative Oracle (5a-5d) first, then 4b scheduling, then parallel daemons. Ships the differentiator first — the feature no other tool has. Effort: M overall. Risk: Low (Oracle is isolated, doesn't touch daemon lifecycle).
options.preview:
APPROACH B: Brain-First
  Sequence: Oracle Memory (5a) → Reflection (5b)
           → Domain Research (5c) → Taste (5d)
           → Cron Scheduling (4b)
           → Parallel Daemons
  Effort:  M (human: ~3 weeks / CC: ~6 hours)
  Risk:    Low — Oracle changes are isolated
  Pros:    • Ships the moat first
           • Oracle improves ALL subsequent daemon runs
           • Validated by 2026 SOTA (file-based memory)
  Cons:    • No scheduling until Oracle ships
           • Daemon stays manual-trigger for longer
  Reuses:  oracle.ts, job-runner.ts completion hooks

options.label: A) Infrastructure-First
options.description: 4b scheduling first, then parallel daemons, then Creative Oracle. Ships the 'just works' daemon features first. Effort: S-M overall. Risk: Low.
options.preview:
APPROACH A: Infrastructure-First
  Sequence: Cron Scheduling (4b) → Config Reload
           → Parallel Daemons
           → Creative Oracle (5a-5d)
  Effort:  S-M (human: ~2 weeks / CC: ~4 hours)
  Risk:    Low — incremental additions
  Pros:    • Daemon becomes useful fastest
           • Smallest first step (4b is XS)
           • Parallel daemons inform Oracle design
  Cons:    • Oracle (the differentiator) ships last
           • Stateless decisions for longer
  Reuses:  triggers.ts, daemon.ts, job-runner.ts

options.label: C) Unified Platform
options.description: Design all three together, ship as one integrated release. Cleanest architecture but longest time-to-first-ship. Effort: L. Risk: Medium (large blast radius).
options.preview:
APPROACH C: Unified Platform
  Sequence: Shared types + config schema
           → All three in parallel design
           → Integrated implementation
           → Single release
  Effort:  L (human: ~4 weeks / CC: ~8 hours)
  Risk:    Medium — large diff, complex testing
  Pros:    • Cleanest architecture from day 1
           • No retrofitting between phases
           • Shared config design
  Cons:    • Longest time to any value
           • Harder to test incrementally
           • Big-bang risk
  Reuses:  Everything, but all at once

rationale: CEO phase means P1+P2 dominate — Brain-First ships the differentiator (the feature no other tool has) first while keeping blast radius small, and its isolation means scheduling/parallel work can layer on cleanly after without rework.

question:
GaryClaw on main, reviewing the 3-phase roadmap (Oracle → Scheduling → Parallel). What review posture do you want? You already have detailed draft designs — this is about whether to push scope further or lock in what you have.

RECOMMENDATION: Choose SELECTIVE EXPANSION — the drafts are solid, but I spotted expansion opportunities worth surfacing (cross-project memory, Oracle self-improvement, taste inference). You cherry-pick what's worth adding. Completeness: EXPANSION=9/10, SELECTIVE=8/10, HOLD=7/10, REDUCTION=5/10.

options.label: SELECTIVE EXPANSION (Recommended)
options.description: Hold current scope as baseline, but surface expansion opportunities individually for cherry-picking. Neutral recommendations — you decide what's worth adding.

options.label: SCOPE EXPANSION
options.description: Dream big. Push scope UP. 10x check, platonic ideal, delight opportunities. Every expansion presented for opt-in.

options.label: HOLD SCOPE
options.description: The drafts are right. Maximum rigor on architecture, security, edge cases, observability. No expansions surfaced.

options.label: SCOPE REDUCTION
options.description: The roadmap is overbuilt. Find the minimum viable version and cut everything else.

rationale: The drafts are solid infrastructure — selective expansion lets us cherry-pick high-value additions like cross-project memory and taste inference without bloating scope, consistent with the brain-first sequencing we already committed to.

question:
EXPANSION 1: Cross-project memory sharing. Right now, the Creative Oracle's memory lives in `.garyclaw/oracle-memory/` per project. But domain expertise about 'autonomous agent architectures' learned in GaryClaw would be valuable in other agent projects. This would add a global `~/.garyclaw/oracle-memory/` layer that any project can read, with per-project overrides. 

Effort: S (human: ~3 days / CC: ~30 min). Risk: Low — additive, no breaking changes. Already called out as Open Question #1 in the Creative Oracle design.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Design the two-layer memory model (global + per-project) as part of Phase 5a. ~30 extra lines in oracle-memory.ts.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Note as a future enhancement. Ship per-project memory first, add global layer later.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not valuable enough to track.

rationale: It's low-effort (~30 lines), additive with no breaking changes, already identified as an open question in the design, and cross-project memory sharing is a natural completeness win for an autonomous agent that learns domain expertise.

question:
EXPANSION 2: Taste inference from decision patterns. The Oracle observes your decisions over time — after 50+ decisions, it notices patterns like 'you consistently choose the explicit approach over the clever one' or 'you always pick the complete option.' It suggests updates to taste.md: 'I noticed you prefer X over Y — should I add this to your taste profile?' You approve or reject each suggestion.

This is Open Question #2 in the Creative Oracle design. The risk noted there is 'Oracle gaming its own preferences' — but with human approval on every taste update, that risk is mitigated.

Effort: S (human: ~3 days / CC: ~30 min). Risk: Low — suggestions are human-gated, never auto-applied.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add taste inference as Phase 5e — runs after 5b reflection, analyzes decision patterns, proposes taste.md updates with human approval.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Track for later. Ship taste.md as human-authored only, add inference later.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not valuable — human-authored taste is sufficient.

rationale: Human-gated taste inference closes the loop on the Oracle's learning cycle — it's in blast radius of the Creative Oracle design, low risk, and small effort, so it should ship with the phase.

question:
EXPANSION 3: Decision quality tracking. After each job, compare Oracle decisions against outcomes — did the chosen approach actually work? Track a rolling 'decision accuracy' metric: decisions that led to successful fixes vs. ones that needed revision. Display in `daemon status` and notification summaries. Over time, this gives you a trust score for the Oracle.

This is the missing feedback loop — reflection (5b) updates memory, but nothing measures whether the memory is actually making decisions *better*.

Effort: S (human: ~2 days / CC: ~20 min). Risk: Low — read-only metrics, no behavior changes.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add decision accuracy tracking to Phase 5b reflection. Store metrics in oracle-memory/metrics.json, display in daemon status.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Ship reflection first, add metrics later once there's enough decision data to be meaningful.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Not valuable — the reflection narrative is sufficient.

rationale: The feedback loop completes the reflection system — without measuring whether memory-backed decisions actually improve outcomes, you're flying blind on Oracle quality, and it's only ~20 min CC effort squarely in the blast radius of Phase 5b.

question:
EXPANSION 4: Memory-informed scheduling. Once the Oracle has memory, it can suggest *when* to run skills. Example: 'QA finds 3x more bugs after large commits — I should trigger QA after commits touching 5+ files, not on every push.' The daemon learns optimal trigger patterns from job outcomes rather than relying on static cron rules.

This would make Phase 4b's cron triggers a fallback rather than the primary scheduling mechanism.

Effort: M (human: ~1 week / CC: ~1 hour). Risk: Medium — requires enough job history to learn from, cold-start problem.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Design adaptive scheduling as Phase 4b+ — cron as baseline, memory-informed trigger suggestions layered on top.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Ship static cron first. Add adaptive scheduling once there’s enough job history (50+ jobs) to learn from.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Static cron is sufficient. Don't over-engineer scheduling.

rationale: Medium effort + cold-start problem means this can't ship until there's enough job history anyway — ship static cron now, layer adaptive scheduling once 50+ jobs provide real data to learn from.

question:
EXPANSION 5: Memory conflict resolution protocol. Open Question #4 in the Creative Oracle design: when domain expertise says 'X is SOTA' but decision-outcomes shows 'X failed for us,' which wins? Rather than leaving this ambiguous, codify it as a 7th Decision Principle: 'Local evidence trumps general knowledge — if we tried X and it failed, prefer alternatives even if X is theoretically SOTA.'

This resolves the ambiguity before it causes a bad decision at 2am.

Effort: XS (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min). Risk: None — it's a principle addition, not a code change.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add the conflict resolution principle to oracle.ts and the Creative Oracle design. ~10 lines of prompt text.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Note the ambiguity, resolve it after seeing real-world conflicts.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: The Oracle will figure it out from context.

rationale: XS effort, zero risk, and it resolves a known ambiguity before it causes a bad autonomous decision — this is exactly the kind of blast-radius cleanup that should be done now.

question:
EXPANSION 6: Research freshness policy. Open Question #3 in the Creative Oracle design: how often should domain-expertise.md be refreshed? Rather than leaving it vague, codify a staleness check: domain-expertise.md has a `last_researched` timestamp. If older than 14 days AND the Oracle encounters a low-confidence decision in that domain, trigger an automatic refresh before the next job. Otherwise, manual via `garyclaw research <topic>`.

This prevents both 'stale knowledge' and 'unnecessary re-research.'

Effort: XS (human: ~2 hours / CC: ~10 min). Risk: None — simple timestamp check.

options.label: A) Add to scope
options.description: Add staleness check to Phase 5c (Domain Research). Timestamp in domain-expertise.md frontmatter, 14-day default, configurable.

options.label: B) Defer to TODOS.md
options.description: Ship manual-only research first, add automatic freshness check later.

options.label: C) Skip
options.description: Manual refresh is sufficient.

rationale: XS effort, zero risk, resolves Open Question #3 before it causes stale knowledge to produce a bad autonomous decision at 2am — same pattern as Expansion 5.
